title: Servant Auth Roles
content_html:
SOLOMON'S BLOG

functional programming, permaculture, math

I wanted a nice way to create a roles system on top of servant-auth. This post walks through the design and implementation process. The end result is quite similar to OCharle's Who Authorized These Ghosts. You can see the final result here.

The immediate thing I wanted was auth roles/permission sets in my Servant API types such that I can define distinct handlers for each auth role. I also want to maintain compatibility with AuthProtect.

The idea

My first move was to sketch out an imaginary interface for the library. My hope is that starting from the interface that the code would reveal its implementation to me.

I wanted to define my roles as a sum type and then instantiate a typeclass to describe the role check conditions. This way I could design a variety of role systems (hierarchical, non-hierarchical, set based, etc).

data UserRole = Viewer | Editor | Admin
  deriving (Eq, Ord, Show)

instance CheckRole 'Viewer where
  checkRole role = role >= Viewer

instance CheckRole 'Editor where
  checkRole role = role >= Editor

instance CheckRole 'Admin where
  checkRole role = role >= Admin

CheckRole allows you to use arbitrary predicates. In this case I use Ord, because I want fall-through but we could just as easily use Eq, or even set membership if you want to model something more like "permissions" then "roles."

Then we would define our typical Servant auth type and AuthServerData instance:

data Authz = Authz { userRole :: UserRole, userName :: String }
  deriving (Show)

type instance AuthServerData (AuthProtect "test-auth") = Authz

And finally some magical Servant combinator RequireRole I can use in my API types to assign auth roles to routes. The combinator would introduce a role permission check before firing the handler. If the UserRole value from Authz doesn't satisfy the RoleChecK for a handler it fails and tries the next matching route.

type PanelAdminAPI =
  RequireRole "test-auth" 'Admin
    :> "panel"
    :> Get '[JSON] String

type PanelEditorAPI =
  RequireRole "test-auth" 'Editor
    :> "panel"
    :> Get '[JSON] String

type PanelViewerAPI =
  RequireRole "test-auth" 'Viewer
    :> "panel"
    :> Get '[JSON] String

type API = PanelAdminAPI :<|> PanelEditorAPI :<|> PanelViewerAPI

server :: Server API
server = panelAdmin :<|> panelEditor :<|> panelViewer
  where
    panelAdmin :: Authz -> Handler String
    panelAdmin _ = pure "admin panel"

    panelEditor :: Authz -> Handler String
    panelEditor _ = pure "editor panel"

    panelViewer :: Authz -> Handler String
    panelViewer _ = pure "viewer panel"

This obviously doens't work as is, but I wanted a DX roughly like this.

Making the idea work

The key to this idea is going to be the RequireRole HasServer instance.

HasServer in a nutshell

But before that, allow me to briefly explain the HasServer class. This isn't a Servant tutorial so I'm going to skip a lot of details.

It has one associated type and two methods:

class HasServer api context where
  type ServerT api (m :: Type -> Type) :: Type

  route :: Proxy api -> Context context -> Delayed env (Server api) -> Router env

  hoistServerWithContext
    :: Proxy api -> Proxy context -> (forall x. m x -> n x) -> ServerT api m -> ServerT api n

type Server api = ServerT api Handler

api is our Servant Combinator. It describes a part of an API's structure. The HasServer instance is sort of a bridge between that API description and an actual route handler that serves it.

The associated type ServerT tells the compiler how to translate from the api type to a fragment of your handler's type signature.

For example the ServerT associated type for Verb is:

type ServerT (Verb method status ctypes a) m = m a

This means Verb 'GET 200 '[JSON] a maps to m a in your handler's signature.

route builds a dispatch tree called a Router that Servant then converts via serve into a WAI Application for processing requests.

Each API component has its own route definition and they recursively build up a Delayed computation used to produce the input to the handler function.

For example, Capture "id" Int says to grab the next URL path segment, try to parse it as an Int, then pass it to the handler. Chaining more API components builds out the Delayed computation whose result is passed to the handler function.

Lastly we have hoistServerWithContext. Servant applications are typically written in a custom monad m but ultimately they need to be run in Handler. We use a natural transformation to map from m to Handler. This function says how to propagate the natural transformation through this API component.

Our instance

Our datatype will contain a symbol representing the auth method to lookup in the Context and a role r that we will use to check against our authenticated user's role:

data RequireRole (tag :: Symbol) (r :: k)

Our hoistServerWithContext definition is boring and just passes the natural transformation through the next API component.

route is where we do all the work. We want to get the AuthServerData from the AuthHandler and call checkRole to compare the user's role against the required role in the API component.

To make this work we need to create another typeclass that tells us how to extract a role from our auth context.

class HasRole auth r | auth -> r where
  getRole :: auth -> r

instance HasRole Authz UserRole where
  getRole = userRole

Then we fancy up CheckRole with an associated type and a Proxy:

class CheckRole (r :: k) where
  type RoleType r :: Type

  checkRole :: Proxy r -> RoleType r -> Bool

Now inside route we can use Proxy to specialize CheckRole to the instance r and call checkRole against the user's role from getRole:

checkRole (Proxy @r) (getRole auth)

If the role check fails we throw a 403 error. Otherwise we allow the Delayed computation to continue with the AuthServerData.

We call execute the failure with delayedFail. This makes the error non fatal allowing Servant to try the next :<|> alternative. This gives us fall through behavior for matching route paths with different role requirements.

An actual auth lookup failure calls delayedFailFatal which returns immediately. This means we reject all unauthenticated requests.

We could instead ouse delayedFail here allowing an unauthenticated request to fall-through every gate before failing. This would let us create an unauthenticated fallback route.

This is a design choice, perhaps I should offer combinators with both behaviors?

The full HasServer instance:

instance
  forall tag r api context.
  ( HasServer api context,
    CheckRole r,
    HasRole (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag)) (RoleType r),
    HasContextEntry context (AuthHandler Request (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag)))
  ) =>
  HasServer (RequireRole tag r :> api) context
  where
  type
    ServerT (RequireRole tag r :> api) m =
      AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag) -> ServerT api m

  hoistServerWithContext _ pc nt s =
    hoistServerWithContext (Proxy @api) pc nt . s

  route _ context subserver =
    route (Proxy @api) context (subserver `addAuthCheck` withRequest authCheck)
    where
      authHandler' :: Request -> Handler (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag))
      authHandler' = unAuthHandler (getContextEntry context)

      authCheck :: Request -> DelayedIO (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag))
      authCheck req = do
        eResult <- liftIO $ runHandler (authHandler' req)
        case eResult of
          Left err -> delayedFailFatal err
          Right auth ->
            if checkRole (Proxy @r) (getRole auth)
              then pure auth
              else
                delayedFail
                  err403
                    { errBody = "Forbidden: insufficient permissions",
                      errHeaders = [("Content-Type", "text/plain; charset=utf-8")]
                    }

Armed with a working servant combinator we can build a working example.

data UserRole = Viewer | Editor | Admin
  deriving (Ord)

instance CheckRole 'Viewer where
  type RoleType 'Viewer = UserRole
  checkRole _ role = role >= Viewer

instance CheckRole 'Editor where
  type RoleType 'Editor = UserRole
  checkRole _ role = role >= Editor

instance CheckRole 'Admin where
  type RoleType 'Admin = UserRole
  checkRole _ role = role >= Admin

data Authz = Authz { userRole :: UserRole, userName :: String }
  deriving (Show)

instance HasRole Authz UserRole where
  getRole = userRole

type instance AuthServerData (AuthProtect "test-auth") = Authz

type PanelAdminAPI =
  RequireRole "test-auth" 'Admin
    :> "panel"
    :> Get '[JSON] String

type PanelEditorAPI =
  RequireRole "test-auth" 'Editor
    :> "panel"
    :> Get '[JSON] String

type PanelViewerAPI =
  RequireRole "test-auth" 'Viewer
    :> "panel"
    :> Get '[JSON] String

type API = PanelAdminAPI :<|> PanelEditorAPI :<|> PanelViewerAPI

server :: Server API
server = panelAdmin :<|> panelEditor :<|> panelViewer
  where
    panelAdmin :: Authz -> Handler String
    panelAdmin _ = pure "admin panel"

    panelEditor :: Authz -> Handler String
    panelEditor _ = pure "editor panel"

    panelViewer :: Authz -> Handler String
    panelViewer _ = pure "viewer panel"

Nice!

Who Authorized Those Ghosts?

This is pretty neat but I think we can do better. We are gating access to handlers by user roles but we can call any sort of subroutine from our handlers.

If we constructed a special Proof token during the role check we could constrain what subroutines are callable with a handler.

The Proof token would be index by the auth role checked in the HasServer route function. Then we could build functions like:

banUser :: Proof 'Admin -> Authz -> String
banUser _proof auth = "banned by " <> userName auth

And we would only be able to call this function from an 'Admin authscoped handler.

Note that because the Authz itself is not (yet!) indexed by the role, we don't have a proof that a particular user passed the role check, just that some user did.

To make this work we need to define a couple data types:

data Proof (required :: k) = Proof

data Satisfies (required :: k) authz = Satisfies (Proof required) authz

Proof is a witness that some role satifies the requirement required. The constructor is not exported so the only way to construct it will be via a new function checkAuth called in authCheck and yields a Proof if the checkRole succeeds.

Satisfies is an authentication result paired with a proof that it satisfies required. This is what handlers receive.

checkAuth replaces the if statement in authCheck and produces our Proof:

checkAuth :: CheckRole required => Proxy required -> RoleType required -> Maybe (Proof required)
checkAuth p role
  | checkRole p role = Just Proof
  | otherwise = Nothing

authCheck now returns Satisfies required (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag)):

authCheck :: Request -> DelayedIO (Satisfies required (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag)))
authCheck request = do
  eResult <- liftIO $ runHandler (authHandler' request)
  case eResult of
    Left err -> delayedFailFatal err
    Right auth ->
      case checkAuth (Proxy @required) (getRole auth) of
        Just proof -> pure $ Satisfies proof auth
        Nothing ->
          delayedFail
            err403
              { errBody = "Forbidden: insufficient permissions",
                errHeaders = [("Content-Type", "text/plain; charset=utf-8")]
              }

Our handlers now receive Satisfies required (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag)) allowing the admin handler to call banUser using the proof:

adminHandler :: Satisfies 'Admin Authz -> Handler String
adminHandler (Satisfies proof auth) = pure (banUser proof auth)

The other handlers cannot call banUser as they have the wrong proof, demonstrated by their Satisfies param:

editorHandler :: Satisfies 'Editor Authz -> Handler String
editorHandler (Satisfies _proof auth) = pure $ "editor: " <> show (userName auth)

viewerHandler :: Satisfies 'Viewer Authz -> Handler String
viewerHandler (Satisfies _proof auth) = pure $ "viewer: " <> show (userName auth)

We have restricted subroutines to handlers with the approriate role check. What we have not done is restrict those subroutine to users who have satisifed those role checks. Inside editorHandler we could fetch a Authz for some other user and use it with the Proof 'Admin we have acquired.

To prevent this we need to lift the UserRole from a value inside the Authz into an index on UserRole:

newtype Authz (r :: UserRole) = Authz {userName :: String}

In other words, currently the Proof and the subject are independent. We have proven someone is an admin (or whatever) but we don't have evidence of who.

It turns out this will require taking a few steps into the hasochism.

(Don't fear) The Reaper

The remainder gets a little brutal. I'm going to walk through the minimal singletons based solution without all the bells and whistles and with a bunch of boilerplate. In the actual library servant-auth-roles I take a slightly different singletons approach and hide it all behind Template Haskell to eliminate the boilerplate.

Reification and forgery

The foundational concept in singletons is that we construct a GADT that uniquely maps to indices of an indexed type. This allows you to recover type level information at runtime via pattern matching on the GADT.

data SBool (b :: Bool) where
  STrue  :: SBool 'True
  SFalse :: SBool 'False

With SBool, each branch of the GADT uniquely specializes b to a type of the Bool kind. This means that when pattern matching GHC is able to infer b based on the branch.

This allows us to perform reification, mapping from a value to a type.

Imagine trying to write this function:

toSBool :: Bool -> SBool b

No matter which case of SBool you return, you will get a type error because b is universally quantified. However, if we hide the parameter behind an existential quantifier then we can write:

data SomeSBool where
  SomeSBool :: SBool b -> SomeSBool

toSBool :: Bool -> SomeSBool
toSBool True  = SomeSBool STrue
toSBool False = SomeSBool SFalse

And now when we pattern match on SomeBool we can access the specialized b. This allows us to write a function like:

fireTheMissiles :: SBool 'True -> IO ()
fireTheMissiles STrue = print "Missiles have been fired!"

check :: Bool -> IO ()
check input = case toSBool input of
  SomeSBool tru  -> fireTheMissiles tru
  SomeSBool SFalse -> pure ()

Calling fireTheMissiles with SFalse will result in a type error as it demands SBool 'True. This is the vibe of the trick we want to play. However, at this point could forge an STrue in the SFalse branch:

SomeSBool SFalse  -> fireTheMissiles STrue

To prevent a forgery we need to introduce an evidence type that shares the index b. This way we can enforce that our SBool matches that of our evidence.

data IsTrue (b :: Bool) where
  IsTrue :: IsTrue 'True

decide :: SBool b -> Maybe (IsTrue b)
decide STrue  = Just IsTrue
decide SFalse = Nothing

fireTheMissiles :: IsTrue b -> SBool b -> IO ()
fireTheMissiles = ...

The only way to call fireTheMissiles is if our SBool b matches our IsTrue b:

check :: Bool -> IO ()
check input = case toSBool input of
  SomeSBool sb -> case decide sb of
    Just ev -> fireTheMissiles ev sb
    Nothing -> pure ()

Now we can only call fireTheMissiles on input if we have also produced evidence that input is True, which we do with decide.

Let's look at our banUser function:

banUser :: Proof 'Admin -> Authz -> String

Proof 'Admin is our evidence type. If we parameterize it and Authz with r :: UserRole then banUser has exactly the same shape as fireTheMissiles:

banUser :: Proof 'Admin r -> Authz r -> String

We can only call banUser with an Authz that matches Proof.

Applying singletons

Extrapolating from the SBool example, we have three key changes:

  1. We need to dispatch our check function on a type, since the required role is a parameter of RequireRole rather than a fixed value like 'True.
  2. We need to construct the proof inside the HasServer route method.
  3. We need the proof and the Authz it was checked against to travel together from route to our handler.

Dispatch

We need to update checkRole to follow the shape of decide. However, since the typeclass is polymorphic in the role kind we cannot expect a particular singleton GADT. We need to dispatch a singleton on the kind of the RoleType associated type.

For that we introduce the Sing type family.

type family Sing :: k -> Type

Put simply, it is a type level function that allows us to uniquely pick out a singleton type for a kind.

Sing dispatches on the kind of its parameter. So given a SUserRole GADT and instance:

data SUserRole (r :: UserRole) where
  SViewer :: SUserRole 'Viewer
  SEditor :: SUserRole 'Editor
  SAdmin :: SUserRole 'Admin

type instance Sing = SUserRole

Sing (r :: UserRole) must dispatch to SUserRole. If there are instances for multiple GADTs on the same kind then GHC will produce a conflicting instance error.

Now in our checkRole method we replace RoleType required with its singleton via Sing.

class CheckRole (required :: k) where
  -- | The value-level type that this role checks against.
  type RoleType required :: Type

  -- | Check whether the given role value satisfies the requirement @required@.
  checkRole :: Sing (actual :: RoleType required) -> ...

For CheckRole 'Viewer, RoleType 'Viewer resolves to UserRole giving us Sing (actual :: UserRole) which we just showed returns SUserRole.

Evidence

Now we must update our Proof type and complete the signature for checkRole. Proof currently carries the required auth role.

Recall that in our current version the user provides checkRole :: Proxy required -> RoleType required -> Bool and then the library function checkAuth acts as a smart constructor to build the appropriate Proof:

checkAuth :: CheckRole required => Proxy required -> RoleType required -> Maybe (Proof required)
checkAuth p role
  | checkRole p role = Just Proof
  | otherwise = Nothing

This allows us to keep the Proof data constructor out of user space to eliminate forgery.

In the new world we don't need this.

Lets take a step back and think about what proof we need.

  1. The role the route demands.
  2. The role the user has.

1 is how we dispatch a proof to a handler and 2 is the actual role of the authorized user, which ties the proof to the subject it was checked against.

data Proof (required :: kr) (actual :: ka) where
  Proof :: Proof required actual

required is the role required by the route and actual is the auth'd user's role.

Now we can complete our checkRole signature, dropping the proxy.

checkRole :: Sing (actual :: RoleType required) -> Maybe (Proof required actual)

And we can write an instance like this:

instance CheckRole 'Editor where
  checkRole SViewer = Nothing
  checkRole SEditor = Just Proof
  checkRole SAdmin  = Just Proof

The required role is 'Editor so Sing (RoleType required) evaluates to SUserRole _ representing the actual role of the authenticated user. If it is SViewer then we reject with Nothing, otherwise we produce a Just Proof which is specialized to Proof 'Editor actual.

In the SEditor branch, actual is specialized to 'Editor giving us Proof 'Editor 'Editor.

Now, were we to construct a proof in the SViewer branch it would be Proof 'Editor 'Viewer. This would be morally wrong as the point of the proof is that the actual satisfies the role requirement. We need to eliminate the ability to produce such a proof.

We can do that with another type family.

type family Satisfied (required :: kr) (actual :: ka) :: Constraint

Satisfied is the constraint that holds when actual meets the satisfaction condition for required. That condition is user defined (equality, set membership, order, etc) so I'm going to defer an example for a moment.

Now we attach that constraint on Proof and we can gaurantee that the satisfaction condition holds when constructing a proof.


data Proof (required :: kr) (actual :: ka) where
  Proof :: (Satisfied required actual) => Proof required actual

Now checkRole SViewer = Just Proof would demand Satisfied 'Editor 'Viewer which wont typecheck.

This also means we no longer have to worry about hiding the Proof data constructor. There is no way for a user to construct a proof whose actual role fails to satisfy the requirement. Thus we can also eliminate the checkAuth library function.

Constructing

Now we need to update our HasServer instance using the new CheckRole class. To do this we need to reify the role type from the request value.

We do this with another GADT SomeRole which works just like SomeSBool.

data SomeRole (authF :: k -> Type) where
  SomeRole :: Sing (r :: k) -> authF r -> SomeRole authF

The only real difference is that we also pack SomeRole with the auth value along with the singleton. We construct the SomeRole value in our AuthHandler and then discharge it in the HasServer instance.

Introduction:

parseRole :: Request -> Maybe (SomeRole Authz)
parseRole req = case lookup "X-Role" (requestHeaders req) of
  Just "viewer" -> pure $ SomeRole SViewer (Authz "Reed")
  Just "editor" -> pure $ SomeRole SEditor (Authz "Lyxia")
  Just "admin" -> pure $ SomeRole SAdmin (Authz "Sandy")
  _ -> Nothing

-- | Reads the "X-Role" header to determine the user's role.
-- Missing header -> 401. Values: "viewer", "editor", "admin".
roleAuthHandler :: AuthHandler Request (SomeRole Authz)
roleAuthHandler = mkAuthHandler $ \req ->
  maybe (throwError err401) pure (parseRole req)

Elimination:

authCheck :: Request -> DelayedIO (Satisfies required authF)
authCheck request = do
  eResult <- liftIO $ runHandler (authHandler' request)
  case eResult of
    Left err -> delayedFailFatal err
    Right (SomeRole sActual auth) ->
      case checkRole sActual of
        Just proof -> pure $ Satisfies proof auth
        Nothing ->
          delayedFail
            err403
              { errBody = "Forbidden: insufficient permissions",
                errHeaders = [("Content-Type", "text/plain; charset=utf-8")]
              }

Satisfies becomes the final GADT which carries the associated Proof the auth object to be propagated to our handler.

data Satisfies (required :: kr) (authF :: ka -> Type) where
  Satisfies :: Proof required actual -> authF actual -> Satisfies required authF

Discharging

We still need to define our Satisfied instance for UserRole.

type family (a :: UserRole) <=? (b :: UserRole) :: Bool where
  'Viewer <=? _       = 'True
  'Editor <=? 'Viewer = 'False
  'Editor <=? _       = 'True
  'Admin  <=? 'Admin  = 'True
  'Admin  <=? _       = 'False

type instance Satisfied (required :: UserRole) (actual :: UserRole) =
  (required <=? actual) ~ 'True

<=? is a type level Ord specialized to UserRole. Viewer matches anything, Editor matches itself or Admin, Admin matches itself. Then in Satisfied, if required <=? actual evaluates to 'True then the whole constraint evalues to 'True ~ 'True and holds.

And at last we are back to our servant handlers. They receive a Satisfies 'Admin Authz which contains the Proof and the Authz both at the same r, so the proof can only be spent on the very Authz it was checked against.

This allows us to write banUser such that it can only be used in authorized route by an authorized user.

banUser :: Proof 'Admin r -> Authz r -> String
banUser _prf auth = "banned by " <> userName auth

Conclusion

Wow that was brutal. Writing dependent Haskell is painful. Luckily a ton of this code is boilerplate that can be automated away via template haskell, which i have done in my libary servant-auth-roles.

The complete UserRole example becomes:

data UserRole = Viewer | Editor | Admin

$(deriveOrdRole ''UserRole)

newtype Authz (r :: UserRole) = Authz {userName :: String}

type instance AuthServerData (AuthProtect "role-auth") = SomeRole Authz

parseRole :: Request -> Maybe (SomeRole Authz)
parseRole req = case lookup "X-Role" (requestHeaders req) of
  Just "viewer" -> Just (someUserRole Viewer (Authz "Reed"))
  Just "editor" -> Just (someUserRole Editor (Authz "Lyxia"))
  Just "admin" -> Just (someUserRole Admin (Authz "Sandy"))
  _ -> Nothing

roleAuthHandler :: AuthHandler Request (SomeRole Authz)
roleAuthHandler = mkAuthHandler $ maybe (throwError err401) pure . parseRole

banUser :: (IsAtleastAdmin userAuth) => Authz userAuth -> String
banUser auth = "banned by " <> userName auth

type PanelAdminAPI = RequireRole "role-auth" 'Admin :> "panel" :> Get '[JSON] String

type PanelEditorAPI = RequireRole "role-auth" 'Editor :> "panel" :> Get '[JSON] String

type PanelViewerAPI = RequireRole "role-auth" 'Viewer :> "panel" :> Get '[JSON] String

type FallthroughAPI = PanelAdminAPI :<|> PanelEditorAPI :<|> PanelViewerAPI

server :: Server FallthroughAPI
server = pAdmin :<|> pEditor :<|> pViewer
  where
    pAdmin :: Satisfies 'Admin Authz -> Handler String
    pAdmin (Satisfies UserRoleProof auth) = pure (banUser auth)

    pEditor :: Satisfies 'Editor Authz -> Handler String
    pEditor _ = pure "editor panel"

    pViewer :: Satisfies 'Viewer Authz -> Handler String
    pViewer _ = pure "viewer panel"

app :: IO Application
app = pure $ serveWithContext (Proxy @FallthroughAPI) (roleAuthHandler :. EmptyContext) server

No singletons or weird boilerplate in sight!

The hierarchy is derived via Ord. Equality based roles and Set Membership based roles can be derived with deriveEqRole and deriveMemberRole.

To make this work we need to create another typeclass that tells us how to extract a role from our auth context. class HasRole auth r | auth -> r where getRole :: auth -> r instance HasRole Authz UserRole where getRole = userRole Then we fancy up CheckRole with an associated type and a Proxy: class CheckRole (r :: k) where type RoleType r :: Type checkRole :: Proxy r -> RoleType r -> Bool Now inside route we can use Proxy to specialize CheckRole to the instance r and call checkRole against the user's role from getRole: checkRole (Proxy @r) (getRole auth) If the role check fails we throw a 403 error. Otherwise we allow the Delayed computation to continue with the AuthServerData. We call execute the failure with delayedFail. This makes the error non fatal allowing Servant to try the next :<|> alternative. This gives us fall through behavior for matching route paths with different role requirements. An actual auth lookup failure calls delayedFailFatal which returns immediately. This means we reject all unauthenticated requests. We could instead ouse delayedFail here allowing an unauthenticated request to fall-through every gate before failing. This would let us create an unauthenticated fallback route. This is a design choice, perhaps I should offer combinators with both behaviors? The full HasServer instance: instance forall tag r api context. ( HasServer api context, CheckRole r, HasRole (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag)) (RoleType r), HasContextEntry context (AuthHandler Request (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag))) ) => HasServer (RequireRole tag r :> api) context where type ServerT (RequireRole tag r :> api) m = AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag) -> ServerT api m hoistServerWithContext _ pc nt s = hoistServerWithContext (Proxy @api) pc nt . s route _ context subserver = route (Proxy @api) context (subserver `addAuthCheck` withRequest authCheck) where authHandler' :: Request -> Handler (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag)) authHandler' = unAuthHandler (getContextEntry context) authCheck :: Request -> DelayedIO (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag)) authCheck req = do eResult <- liftIO $ runHandler (authHandler' req) case eResult of Left err -> delayedFailFatal err Right auth -> if checkRole (Proxy @r) (getRole auth) then pure auth else delayedFail err403 { errBody = "Forbidden: insufficient permissions", errHeaders = [("Content-Type", "text/plain; charset=utf-8")] } Armed with a working servant combinator we can build a working example. data UserRole = Viewer | Editor | Admin deriving (Ord) instance CheckRole 'Viewer where type RoleType 'Viewer = UserRole checkRole _ role = role >= Viewer instance CheckRole 'Editor where type RoleType 'Editor = UserRole checkRole _ role = role >= Editor instance CheckRole 'Admin where type RoleType 'Admin = UserRole checkRole _ role = role >= Admin data Authz = Authz { userRole :: UserRole, userName :: String } deriving (Show) instance HasRole Authz UserRole where getRole = userRole type instance AuthServerData (AuthProtect "test-auth") = Authz type PanelAdminAPI = RequireRole "test-auth" 'Admin :> "panel" :> Get '[JSON] String type PanelEditorAPI = RequireRole "test-auth" 'Editor :> "panel" :> Get '[JSON] String type PanelViewerAPI = RequireRole "test-auth" 'Viewer :> "panel" :> Get '[JSON] String type API = PanelAdminAPI :<|> PanelEditorAPI :<|> PanelViewerAPI server :: Server API server = panelAdmin :<|> panelEditor :<|> panelViewer where panelAdmin :: Authz -> Handler String panelAdmin _ = pure "admin panel" panelEditor :: Authz -> Handler String panelEditor _ = pure "editor panel" panelViewer :: Authz -> Handler String panelViewer _ = pure "viewer panel" Nice! Who Authorized Those Ghosts? This is pretty neat but I think we can do better. We are gating access to handlers by user roles but we can call any sort of subroutine from our handlers. If we constructed a special Proof token during the role check we could constrain what subroutines are callable with a handler. The Proof token would be index by the auth role checked in the HasServer route function. Then we could build functions like: banUser :: Proof 'Admin -> Authz -> String banUser _proof auth = "banned by " <> userName auth And we would only be able to call this function from an 'Admin authscoped handler. Note that because the Authz itself is not (yet!) indexed by the role, we don't have a proof that a particular user passed the role check, just that some user did. To make this work we need to define a couple data types: data Proof (required :: k) = Proof data Satisfies (required :: k) authz = Satisfies (Proof required) authz Proof is a witness that some role satifies the requirement required. The constructor is not exported so the only way to construct it will be via a new function checkAuth called in authCheck and yields a Proof if the checkRole succeeds. Satisfies is an authentication result paired with a proof that it satisfies required. This is what handlers receive. checkAuth replaces the if statement in authCheck and produces our Proof: checkAuth :: CheckRole required => Proxy required -> RoleType required -> Maybe (Proof required) checkAuth p role | checkRole p role = Just Proof | otherwise = Nothing authCheck now returns Satisfies required (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag)): authCheck :: Request -> DelayedIO (Satisfies required (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag))) authCheck request = do eResult <- liftIO $ runHandler (authHandler' request) case eResult of Left err -> delayedFailFatal err Right auth -> case checkAuth (Proxy @required) (getRole auth) of Just proof -> pure $ Satisfies proof auth Nothing -> delayedFail err403 { errBody = "Forbidden: insufficient permissions", errHeaders = [("Content-Type", "text/plain; charset=utf-8")] } Our handlers now receive Satisfies required (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag)) allowing the admin handler to call banUser using the proof: adminHandler :: Satisfies 'Admin Authz -> Handler String adminHandler (Satisfies proof auth) = pure (banUser proof auth) The other handlers cannot call banUser as they have the wrong proof, demonstrated by their Satisfies param: editorHandler :: Satisfies 'Editor Authz -> Handler String editorHandler (Satisfies _proof auth) = pure $ "editor: " <> show (userName auth) viewerHandler :: Satisfies 'Viewer Authz -> Handler String viewerHandler (Satisfies _proof auth) = pure $ "viewer: " <> show (userName auth) We have restricted subroutines to handlers with the approriate role check. What we have not done is restrict those subroutine to users who have satisifed those role checks. Inside editorHandler we could fetch a Authz for some other user and use it with the Proof 'Admin we have acquired. To prevent this we need to lift the UserRole from a value inside the Authz into an index on UserRole: newtype Authz (r :: UserRole) = Authz {userName :: String} In other words, currently the Proof and the subject are independent. We have proven someone is an admin (or whatever) but we don't have evidence of who. It turns out this will require taking a few steps into the hasochism. (Don't fear) The Reaper The remainder gets a little brutal. I'm going to walk through the minimal singletons based solution without all the bells and whistles and with a bunch of boilerplate. In the actual library servant-auth-roles I take a slightly different singletons approach and hide it all behind Template Haskell to eliminate the boilerplate. Reification and forgery The foundational concept in singletons is that we construct a GADT that uniquely maps to indices of an indexed type. This allows you to recover type level information at runtime via pattern matching on the GADT. data SBool (b :: Bool) where STrue :: SBool 'True SFalse :: SBool 'False With SBool, each branch of the GADT uniquely specializes b to a type of the Bool kind. This means that when pattern matching GHC is able to infer b based on the branch. This allows us to perform reification, mapping from a value to a type. Imagine trying to write this function: toSBool :: Bool -> SBool b No matter which case of SBool you return, you will get a type error because b is universally quantified. However, if we hide the parameter behind an existential quantifier then we can write: data SomeSBool where SomeSBool :: SBool b -> SomeSBool toSBool :: Bool -> SomeSBool toSBool True = SomeSBool STrue toSBool False = SomeSBool SFalse And now when we pattern match on SomeBool we can access the specialized b. This allows us to write a function like: fireTheMissiles :: SBool 'True -> IO () fireTheMissiles STrue = print "Missiles have been fired!" check :: Bool -> IO () check input = case toSBool input of SomeSBool tru -> fireTheMissiles tru SomeSBool SFalse -> pure () Calling fireTheMissiles with SFalse will result in a type error as it demands SBool 'True. This is the vibe of the trick we want to play. However, at this point could forge an STrue in the SFalse branch: SomeSBool SFalse -> fireTheMissiles STrue To prevent a forgery we need to introduce an evidence type that shares the index b. This way we can enforce that our SBool matches that of our evidence. data IsTrue (b :: Bool) where IsTrue :: IsTrue 'True decide :: SBool b -> Maybe (IsTrue b) decide STrue = Just IsTrue decide SFalse = Nothing fireTheMissiles :: IsTrue b -> SBool b -> IO () fireTheMissiles = ... The only way to call fireTheMissiles is if our SBool b matches our IsTrue b: check :: Bool -> IO () check input = case toSBool input of SomeSBool sb -> case decide sb of Just ev -> fireTheMissiles ev sb Nothing -> pure () Now we can only call fireTheMissiles on input if we have also produced evidence that input is True, which we do with decide. Let's look at our banUser function: banUser :: Proof 'Admin -> Authz -> String Proof 'Admin is our evidence type. If we parameterize it and Authz with r :: UserRole then banUser has exactly the same shape as fireTheMissiles: banUser :: Proof 'Admin r -> Authz r -> String We can only call banUser with an Authz that matches Proof. Applying singletons Extrapolating from the SBool example, we have three key changes: We need to dispatch our check function on a type, since the required role is a parameter of RequireRole rather than a fixed value like 'True. We need to construct the proof inside the HasServer route method. We need the proof and the Authz it was checked against to travel together from route to our handler. Dispatch We need to update checkRole to follow the shape of decide. However, since the typeclass is polymorphic in the role kind we cannot expect a particular singleton GADT. We need to dispatch a singleton on the kind of the RoleType associated type. For that we introduce the Sing type family. type family Sing :: k -> Type Put simply, it is a type level function that allows us to uniquely pick out a singleton type for a kind. Sing dispatches on the kind of its parameter. So given a SUserRole GADT and instance: data SUserRole (r :: UserRole) where SViewer :: SUserRole 'Viewer SEditor :: SUserRole 'Editor SAdmin :: SUserRole 'Admin type instance Sing = SUserRole Sing (r :: UserRole) must dispatch to SUserRole. If there are instances for multiple GADTs on the same kind then GHC will produce a conflicting instance error. Now in our checkRole method we replace RoleType required with its singleton via Sing. class CheckRole (required :: k) where -- | The value-level type that this role checks against. type RoleType required :: Type -- | Check whether the given role value satisfies the requirement @required@. checkRole :: Sing (actual :: RoleType required) -> ... For CheckRole 'Viewer, RoleType 'Viewer resolves to UserRole giving us Sing (actual :: UserRole) which we just showed returns SUserRole. Evidence Now we must update our Proof type and complete the signature for checkRole. Proof currently carries the required auth role. Recall that in our current version the user provides checkRole :: Proxy required -> RoleType required -> Bool and then the library function checkAuth acts as a smart constructor to build the appropriate Proof: checkAuth :: CheckRole required => Proxy required -> RoleType required -> Maybe (Proof required) checkAuth p role | checkRole p role = Just Proof | otherwise = Nothing This allows us to keep the Proof data constructor out of user space to eliminate forgery. In the new world we don't need this. Lets take a step back and think about what proof we need. The role the route demands. The role the user has. 1 is how we dispatch a proof to a handler and 2 is the actual role of the authorized user, which ties the proof to the subject it was checked against. data Proof (required :: kr) (actual :: ka) where Proof :: Proof required actual required is the role required by the route and actual is the auth'd user's role. Now we can complete our checkRole signature, dropping the proxy. checkRole :: Sing (actual :: RoleType required) -> Maybe (Proof required actual) And we can write an instance like this: instance CheckRole 'Editor where checkRole SViewer = Nothing checkRole SEditor = Just Proof checkRole SAdmin = Just Proof The required role is 'Editor so Sing (RoleType required) evaluates to SUserRole _ representing the actual role of the authenticated user. If it is SViewer then we reject with Nothing, otherwise we produce a Just Proof which is specialized to Proof 'Editor actual. In the SEditor branch, actual is specialized to 'Editor giving us Proof 'Editor 'Editor. Now, were we to construct a proof in the SViewer branch it would be Proof 'Editor 'Viewer. This would be morally wrong as the point of the proof is that the actual satisfies the role requirement. We need to eliminate the ability to produce such a proof. We can do that with another type family. type family Satisfied (required :: kr) (actual :: ka) :: Constraint Satisfied is the constraint that holds when actual meets the satisfaction condition for required. That condition is user defined (equality, set membership, order, etc) so I'm going to defer an example for a moment. Now we attach that constraint on Proof and we can gaurantee that the satisfaction condition holds when constructing a proof. data Proof (required :: kr) (actual :: ka) where Proof :: (Satisfied required actual) => Proof required actual Now checkRole SViewer = Just Proof would demand Satisfied 'Editor 'Viewer which wont typecheck. This also means we no longer have to worry about hiding the Proof data constructor. There is no way for a user to construct a proof whose actual role fails to satisfy the requirement. Thus we can also eliminate the checkAuth library function. Constructing Now we need to update our HasServer instance using the new CheckRole class. To do this we need to reify the role type from the request value. We do this with another GADT SomeRole which works just like SomeSBool. data SomeRole (authF :: k -> Type) where SomeRole :: Sing (r :: k) -> authF r -> SomeRole authF The only real difference is that we also pack SomeRole with the auth value along with the singleton. We construct the SomeRole value in our AuthHandler and then discharge it in the HasServer instance. Introduction: parseRole :: Request -> Maybe (SomeRole Authz) parseRole req = case lookup "X-Role" (requestHeaders req) of Just "viewer" -> pure $ SomeRole SViewer (Authz "Reed") Just "editor" -> pure $ SomeRole SEditor (Authz "Lyxia") Just "admin" -> pure $ SomeRole SAdmin (Authz "Sandy") _ -> Nothing -- | Reads the "X-Role" header to determine the user's role. -- Missing header -> 401. Values: "viewer", "editor", "admin". roleAuthHandler :: AuthHandler Request (SomeRole Authz) roleAuthHandler = mkAuthHandler $ \req -> maybe (throwError err401) pure (parseRole req) Elimination: authCheck :: Request -> DelayedIO (Satisfies required authF) authCheck request = do eResult <- liftIO $ runHandler (authHandler' request) case eResult of Left err -> delayedFailFatal err Right (SomeRole sActual auth) -> case checkRole sActual of Just proof -> pure $ Satisfies proof auth Nothing -> delayedFail err403 { errBody = "Forbidden: insufficient permissions", errHeaders = [("Content-Type", "text/plain; charset=utf-8")] } Satisfies becomes the final GADT which carries the associated Proof the auth object to be propagated to our handler. data Satisfies (required :: kr) (authF :: ka -> Type) where Satisfies :: Proof required actual -> authF actual -> Satisfies required authF Discharging We still need to define our Satisfied instance for UserRole. type family (a :: UserRole) <=? (b :: UserRole) :: Bool where 'Viewer <=? _ = 'True 'Editor <=? 'Viewer = 'False 'Editor <=? _ = 'True 'Admin <=? 'Admin = 'True 'Admin <=? _ = 'False type instance Satisfied (required :: UserRole) (actual :: UserRole) = (required <=? actual) ~ 'True <=? is a type level Ord specialized to UserRole. Viewer matches anything, Editor matches itself or Admin, Admin matches itself. Then in Satisfied, if required <=? actual evaluates to 'True then the whole constraint evalues to 'True ~ 'True and holds. And at last we are back to our servant handlers. They receive a Satisfies 'Admin Authz which contains the Proof and the Authz both at the same r, so the proof can only be spent on the very Authz it was checked against. This allows us to write banUser such that it can only be used in authorized route by an authorized user. banUser :: Proof 'Admin r -> Authz r -> String banUser _prf auth = "banned by " <> userName auth Conclusion Wow that was brutal. Writing dependent Haskell is painful. Luckily a ton of this code is boilerplate that can be automated away via template haskell, which i have done in my libary servant-auth-roles. The complete UserRole example becomes: data UserRole = Viewer | Editor | Admin $(deriveOrdRole ''UserRole) newtype Authz (r :: UserRole) = Authz {userName :: String} type instance AuthServerData (AuthProtect "role-auth") = SomeRole Authz parseRole :: Request -> Maybe (SomeRole Authz) parseRole req = case lookup "X-Role" (requestHeaders req) of Just "viewer" -> Just (someUserRole Viewer (Authz "Reed")) Just "editor" -> Just (someUserRole Editor (Authz "Lyxia")) Just "admin" -> Just (someUserRole Admin (Authz "Sandy")) _ -> Nothing roleAuthHandler :: AuthHandler Request (SomeRole Authz) roleAuthHandler = mkAuthHandler $ maybe (throwError err401) pure . parseRole banUser :: (IsAtleastAdmin userAuth) => Authz userAuth -> String banUser auth = "banned by " <> userName auth type PanelAdminAPI = RequireRole "role-auth" 'Admin :> "panel" :> Get '[JSON] String type PanelEditorAPI = RequireRole "role-auth" 'Editor :> "panel" :> Get '[JSON] String type PanelViewerAPI = RequireRole "role-auth" 'Viewer :> "panel" :> Get '[JSON] String type FallthroughAPI = PanelAdminAPI :<|> PanelEditorAPI :<|> PanelViewerAPI server :: Server FallthroughAPI server = pAdmin :<|> pEditor :<|> pViewer where pAdmin :: Satisfies 'Admin Authz -> Handler String pAdmin (Satisfies UserRoleProof auth) = pure (banUser auth) pEditor :: Satisfies 'Editor Authz -> Handler String pEditor _ = pure "editor panel" pViewer :: Satisfies 'Viewer Authz -> Handler String pViewer _ = pure "viewer panel" app :: IO Application app = pure $ serveWithContext (Proxy @FallthroughAPI) (roleAuthHandler :. EmptyContext) server No singletons or weird boilerplate in sight! The hierarchy is derived via Ord. Equality based roles and Set Membership based roles can be derived with deriveEqRole and deriveMemberRole.

blocks.html_en: The foundational concept in singletons is that we construct a GADT that uniquely maps to indices of an indexed type. This allows you to recover type level information at runtime via pattern matching on the GADT.

sentences.en: This allows you to recover type level information at runtime via pattern matching on the GADT.
sentences.zh: 这允许您在运行时通过 GADT 上的模式匹配恢复类型级别信息。

blocks.html_en: With SBool, each branch of the GADT uniquely specializes b to a type of the Bool kind. This means that when pattern matching GHC is able to infer b based on the branch.

sentences.en: This means that when pattern matching GHC is able to infer b based on the branch.
sentences.zh: 这意味着当模式匹配时，GHC 能够根据分支推断 b。

sentences.en: This allows us to perform reification, mapping from a value to a type.
sentences.zh: 这使我们能够执行具体化，即从值到类型的映射。

content_html_zh:
所罗门的博客

函数式编程、永续农业、数学

我想要一种在servant-auth 之上创建角色系统的好方法。 这篇文章介绍了设计和实现过程。 最终的结果与奥查尔的《谁授权了这些幽灵》非常相似。 您可以在此处查看最终结果。

我最想要的就是我的 Servant API 类型中的身份验证角色/权限集，这样我就可以为每个身份验证角色定义不同的处理程序。 我还想保持与 AuthProtect 的兼容性。

这个想法

我的第一步是为图书馆绘制一个想象中的界面。 我希望从界面开始，代码能够向我揭示其实现。

我想将我的角色定义为总和类型，然后实例化一个类型类来描述角色检查条件。 这样我就可以设计各种角色系统（分层、非分层、基于集合等）。

data UserRole = Viewer | Editor | Admin
  deriving (Eq, Ord, Show)

instance CheckRole 'Viewer where
  checkRole role = role >= Viewer

instance CheckRole 'Editor where
  checkRole role = role >= Editor

instance CheckRole 'Admin where
  checkRole role = role >= Admin

CheckRole 允许您使用任意谓词。 在本例中，我使用 Ord，因为我想要失败，但我们可以轻松使用 Eq，或者如果您想要建模更像“权限”然后是“角色”的东西，甚至可以设置成员资格。

然后我们将定义典型的 Servant 身份验证类型和 AuthServerData 实例：

data Authz = Authz { userRole :: UserRole, userName :: String }
  deriving (Show)

type instance AuthServerData (AuthProtect "test-auth") = Authz

最后，我可以在 API 类型中使用一些神奇的 Servant 组合器 RequireRole 来为路由分配身份验证角色。 组合器将在触发处理程序之前引入角色权限检查。 如果 Authz 中的 UserRole 值不满足处理程序的 RoleCheckK，则处理程序将失败并尝试下一个匹配的路由。

type PanelAdminAPI =
  RequireRole "test-auth" 'Admin
    :> "panel"
    :> Get '[JSON] String

type PanelEditorAPI =
  RequireRole "test-auth" 'Editor
    :> "panel"
    :> Get '[JSON] String

type PanelViewerAPI =
  RequireRole "test-auth" 'Viewer
    :> "panel"
    :> Get '[JSON] String

type API = PanelAdminAPI :<|> PanelEditorAPI :<|> PanelViewerAPI

server :: Server API
server = panelAdmin :<|> panelEditor :<|> panelViewer
  where
    panelAdmin :: Authz -> Handler String
    panelAdmin _ = pure "admin panel"

    panelEditor :: Authz -> Handler String
    panelEditor _ = pure "editor panel"

    panelViewer :: Authz -> Handler String
    panelViewer _ = pure "viewer panel"

这显然不能按原样工作，但我想要一个大致像这样的 DX。

让想法发挥作用

这个想法的关键是 RequireRole HasServer 实例。

HasServer 简而言之

但在此之前，请允许我简要解释一下 HasServer 类。 这不是仆人教程，所以我将跳过很多细节。

它有一种关联类型和两种方法：

class HasServer api context where
  type ServerT api (m :: Type -> Type) :: Type

  route :: Proxy api -> Context context -> Delayed env (Server api) -> Router env

  hoistServerWithContext
    :: Proxy api -> Proxy context -> (forall x. m x -> n x) -> ServerT api m -> ServerT api n

type Server api = ServerT api Handler

api 是我们的 Servant Combinator。 它描述了 API 结构的一部分。 HasServer 实例是 API 描述和为其提供服务的实际路由处理程序之间的桥梁。

关联的类型 ServerT 告诉编译器如何从 api 类型转换为处理程序类型签名的片段。

例如，Verb 的 ServerT 关联类型是：

type ServerT (Verb method status ctypes a) m = m a

这意味着动词“GET 200”[JSON] a 映射到处理程序签名中的 m a。

Route 构建一个名为 Router 的调度树，然后 Servant 通过serve 将其转换为 WAI 应用程序以处理请求。

每个 API 组件都有自己的路由定义，并且它们递归地构建延迟计算，用于生成处理函数的输入。

例如，Capture "id" Int 表示抓取下一个 URL 路径段，尝试将其解析为 Int，然后将其传递给处理程序。 链接更多 API 组件会构建延迟计算，其结果将传递给处理函数。

最后我们有 hoistServerWithContext。 Servant 应用程序通常是用自定义 monad 编写的，但最终它们需要在 Handler 中运行。 我们使用自然变换从 m 映射到 Handler。 该函数说明如何通过该 API 组件传播自然变换。

我们的实例

我们的数据类型将包含一个代表要在上下文中查找的 auth 方法的符号和一个角色 r，我们将使用它来检查经过身份验证的用户的角色：

data RequireRole (tag :: Symbol) (r :: k)

我们的 hoistServerWithContext 定义很无聊，只是通过下一个 API 组件传递自然转换。

路线是我们完成所有工作的地方。 我们希望从 AuthHandler 获取 AuthServerData 并调用 checkRole 将用户的角色与 API 组件中所需的角色进行比较。

为了完成这项工作，我们需要创建另一个类型类，它告诉我们如何从身份验证上下文中提取角色。

class HasRole auth r | auth -> r where
  getRole :: auth -> r

instance HasRole Authz UserRole where
  getRole = userRole

然后我们用关联类型和代理来设计 CheckRole：

class CheckRole (r :: k) where
  type RoleType r :: Type

  checkRole :: Proxy r -> RoleType r -> Bool

现在，在路由内部，我们可以使用 Proxy 将 CheckRole 专门化为实例 r，并从 getRole 中针对用户角色调用 checkRole：

checkRole (Proxy @r) (getRole auth)

如果角色检查失败，我们会抛出 403 错误。 否则，我们允许延迟计算继续使用 AuthServerData。

我们用delayedFail 来调用执行失败。 这使得错误非致命，允许仆人尝试下一个 :<|> 替代方案。 这使我们无法匹配具有不同角色要求的路由路径。

实际的身份验证查找失败会调用delayedFailFatal，它会立即返回。 这意味着我们拒绝所有未经身份验证的请求。

我们可以在这里使用delayedFail，允许未经身份验证的请求在失败之前通过每个门。 这将使我们创建一个未经身份验证的后备路由。

这是一个设计选择，也许我应该提供具有两种行为的组合器？

完整的 HasServer 实例：

instance
  forall tag r api context.
  ( HasServer api context,
    CheckRole r,
    HasRole (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag)) (RoleType r),
    HasContextEntry context (AuthHandler Request (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag)))
  ) =>
  HasServer (RequireRole tag r :> api) context
  where
  type
    ServerT (RequireRole tag r :> api) m =
      AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag) -> ServerT api m

  hoistServerWithContext _ pc nt s =
    hoistServerWithContext (Proxy @api) pc nt . s

  route _ context subserver =
    route (Proxy @api) context (subserver `addAuthCheck` withRequest authCheck)
    where
      authHandler' :: Request -> Handler (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag))
      authHandler' = unAuthHandler (getContextEntry context)

      authCheck :: Request -> DelayedIO (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag))
      authCheck req = do
        eResult <- liftIO $ runHandler (authHandler' req)
        case eResult of
          Left err -> delayedFailFatal err
          Right auth ->
            if checkRole (Proxy @r) (getRole auth)
              then pure auth
              else
                delayedFail
                  err403
                    { errBody = "Forbidden: insufficient permissions",
                      errHeaders = [("Content-Type", "text/plain; charset=utf-8")]
                    }

有了工作仆人组合器，我们就可以构建一个工作示例。

data UserRole = Viewer | Editor | Admin
  deriving (Ord)

instance CheckRole 'Viewer where
  type RoleType 'Viewer = UserRole
  checkRole _ role = role >= Viewer

instance CheckRole 'Editor where
  type RoleType 'Editor = UserRole
  checkRole _ role = role >= Editor

instance CheckRole 'Admin where
  type RoleType 'Admin = UserRole
  checkRole _ role = role >= Admin

data Authz = Authz { userRole :: UserRole, userName :: String }
  deriving (Show)

instance HasRole Authz UserRole where
  getRole = userRole

type instance AuthServerData (AuthProtect "test-auth") = Authz

type PanelAdminAPI =
  RequireRole "test-auth" 'Admin
    :> "panel"
    :> Get '[JSON] String

type PanelEditorAPI =
  RequireRole "test-auth" 'Editor
    :> "panel"
    :> Get '[JSON] String

type PanelViewerAPI =
  RequireRole "test-auth" 'Viewer
    :> "panel"
    :> Get '[JSON] String

type API = PanelAdminAPI :<|> PanelEditorAPI :<|> PanelViewerAPI

server :: Server API
server = panelAdmin :<|> panelEditor :<|> panelViewer
  where
    panelAdmin :: Authz -> Handler String
    panelAdmin _ = pure "admin panel"

    panelEditor :: Authz -> Handler String
    panelEditor _ = pure "editor panel"

    panelViewer :: Authz -> Handler String
    panelViewer _ = pure "viewer panel"

Nice!

谁授权那些鬼魂？

这非常简洁，但我认为我们可以做得更好。 我们通过用户角色来控制对处理程序的访问，但我们可以从处理程序中调用任何类型的子例程。

如果我们在角色检查期间构造一个特殊的证明令牌，我们就可以限制可以使用处理程序调用哪些子例程。

证明令牌将通过 HasServer 路由函数中检查的身份验证角色进行索引。 然后我们可以构建如下函数：

banUser :: Proof 'Admin -> Authz -> String
banUser _proof auth = "banned by " <> userName auth

我们只能从“Admin authscoped 处理程序”调用此函数。

请注意，因为 Authz 本身还不是（还！） 按角色索引，我们没有证据证明特定用户通过了角色检查，只是某个用户通过了。

为了完成这项工作，我们需要定义一些数据类型：

data Proof (required :: k) = Proof

data Satisfies (required :: k) authz = Satisfies (Proof required) authz

证明是某个角色满足所需要求的见证。 构造函数不会导出，因此构造它的唯一方法是通过在 authCheck 中调用的新函数 checkAuth 并在 checkRole 成功时生成 Proof。

满足是一个身份验证结果，并附有满足要求的证明。 这就是处理程序收到的内容。

checkAuth 替换了 authCheck 中的 if 语句并生成我们的证明：

checkAuth :: CheckRole required => Proxy required -> RoleType required -> Maybe (Proof required)
checkAuth p role
  | checkRole p role = Just Proof
  | otherwise = Nothing

authCheck 现在返回满足要求（AuthServerData（AuthProtect 标签））：

authCheck :: Request -> DelayedIO (Satisfies required (AuthServerData (AuthProtect tag)))
authCheck request = do
  eResult <- liftIO $ runHandler (authHandler' request)
  case eResult of
    Left err -> delayedFailFatal err
    Right auth ->
      case checkAuth (Proxy @required) (getRole auth) of
        Just proof -> pure $ Satisfies proof auth
        Nothing ->
          delayedFail
            err403
              { errBody = "Forbidden: insufficient permissions",
                errHeaders = [("Content-Type", "text/plain; charset=utf-8")]
              }

我们的处理程序现在收到满足要求（AuthServerData（AuthProtect 标签）），允许管理处理程序使用证明调用 banUser：

adminHandler :: Satisfies 'Admin Authz -> Handler String
adminHandler (Satisfies proof auth) = pure (banUser proof auth)

其他处理程序无法调用 banUser，因为它们的证明是错误的，如其 Satisfies 参数所示：

editorHandler :: Satisfies 'Editor Authz -> Handler String
editorHandler (Satisfies _proof auth) = pure $ "editor: " <> show (userName auth)

viewerHandler :: Satisfies 'Viewer Authz -> Handler String
viewerHandler (Satisfies _proof auth) = pure $ "viewer: " <> show (userName auth)

我们通过适当的角色检查将子例程限制为处理程序。 我们还没有做的是将这些子例程限制为满足这些角色检查的用户。 在 editorHandler 内部，我们可以为其他用户获取 Authz，并将其与我们获得的 Proof 'Admin 一起使用。

为了防止这种情况，我们需要将 UserRole 从 Authz 内的值提升到 UserRole 上的索引：

newtype Authz (r :: UserRole) = Authz {userName :: String}

换句话说，目前证明和主题是独立的。 我们已经证明某人是管理员（或其他什么），但我们没有证据表明是谁。

事实证明，这将需要采取一些步骤进入受虐狂。

（别害怕）收割者

剩下的部分就有点残酷了。 我将介绍基于最小单例的解决方案，没有所有的花哨和一堆样板。 在实际的库servant-auth-roles中，我采用了稍微不同的单例方法，并将其全部隐藏在Template Haskell后面以消除样板。

具体化和伪造

单例的基本概念是我们构造一个唯一映射到索引类型索引的 GADT。 这允许您在运行时通过 GADT 上的模式匹配恢复类型级别信息。

data SBool (b :: Bool) where
  STrue  :: SBool 'True
  SFalse :: SBool 'False

通过 SBool，GADT 的每个分支都将 b 专门化为 Bool 类型。 这意味着当模式匹配时，GHC 能够根据分支推断 b。

这使我们能够执行具体化，即从值到类型的映射。

想象一下尝试编写这个函数：

toSBool :: Bool -> SBool b

无论返回哪种情况的 SBool，都会出现类型错误，因为 b 是通用量化的。 但是，如果我们将参数隐藏在存在量词后面，那么我们可以写：

data SomeSBool where
  SomeSBool :: SBool b -> SomeSBool

toSBool :: Bool -> SomeSBool
toSBool True  = SomeSBool STrue
toSBool False = SomeSBool SFalse

现在，当我们在 SomeBool 上进行模式匹配时，我们可以访问专门的 b。 这允许我们编写如下函数：

fireTheMissiles :: SBool 'True -> IO ()
fireTheMissiles STrue = print "Missiles have been fired!"

check :: Bool -> IO ()
check input = case toSBool input of
  SomeSBool tru  -> fireTheMissiles tru
  SomeSBool SFalse -> pure ()

使用 SFalse 调用 fireTheMissiles 将导致类型错误，因为它要求 SBool 'True。 这就是我们想要玩的把戏的氛围。 然而，此时可以在 SFalse 分支中伪造 STrue：

SomeSBool SFalse  -> fireTheMissiles STrue

为了防止伪造，我们需要引入共享索引 b 的证据类型。 这样我们就可以强制我们的 SBool 与我们的证据相匹配。

data IsTrue (b :: Bool) where
  IsTrue :: IsTrue 'True

decide :: SBool b -> Maybe (IsTrue b)
decide STrue  = Just IsTrue
decide SFalse = Nothing

fireTheMissiles :: IsTrue b -> SBool b -> IO ()
fireTheMissiles = ...

调用 fireTheMissiles 的唯一方法是我们的​​ SBool b 与 IsTrue b 匹配：

check :: Bool -> IO ()
check input = case toSBool input of
  SomeSBool sb -> case decide sb of
    Just ev -> fireTheMissiles ev sb
    Nothing -> pure ()

现在，如果我们还提供了输入为 True 的证据，那么我们只能在输入上调用 fireTheMissiles，我们将对此进行决定。

让我们看看我们的 banUser 函数：

banUser :: Proof 'Admin -> Authz -> String

Proof 'Admin 是我们的证据类型。 如果我们使用 r :: UserRole 参数化它和 Authz，那么 banUser 的形状与 fireTheMissiles 完全相同：

banUser :: Proof 'Admin r -> Authz r -> String

我们只能使用与 Proof 匹配的 Authz 来调用 banUser。

应用单例

从 SBool 示例推断，我们有三个关键变化：

  1. 我们需要在类型上分派检查函数，因为所需的角色是 RequireRole 的参数，而不是像“True”这样的固定值。
  2. 我们需要在 HasServer 路由方法中构造证明。
  3. 我们需要证据和经过检查的 Authz，以便一起从路线前往我们的处理程序。

派遣

我们需要更新 checkRole 以遵循决定的形状。 然而，由于类型类在角色种类中是多态的，我们不能期望有一个特定的单例 GADT。 我们需要在 RoleType 关联类型的种类上分派一个单例。

为此，我们引入 Sing 类型家族。

type family Sing :: k -> Type

简而言之，它是一个类型级别的函数，允许我们为一种类型唯一地挑选出单例类型。

Sing 调度其参数的类型。 因此，给定一个 SUserRole GADT 和实例：

data SUserRole (r :: UserRole) where
  SViewer :: SUserRole 'Viewer
  SEditor :: SUserRole 'Editor
  SAdmin :: SUserRole 'Admin

type instance Sing = SUserRole

Sing (r :: UserRole) 必须分派给 SUserRole。 如果同一类型存在多个 GADT 的实例，则 GHC 将产生冲突实例错误。

现在，在 checkRole 方法中，我们通过 Sing 将 required RoleType 替换为其单例。

class CheckRole (required :: k) where
  -- | The value-level type that this role checks against.
  type RoleType required :: Type

  -- | Check whether the given role value satisfies the requirement @required@.
  checkRole :: Sing (actual :: RoleType required) -> ...

对于 CheckRole 'Viewer，RoleType 'Viewer 解析为 UserRole，为我们提供 Sing (actual :: UserRole)，我们刚刚显示返回 SUserRole。

证据

现在我们必须更新 Proof 类型并完成 checkRole 的签名。 Proof 目前具有所需的 auth 角色。

回想一下，在我们当前的版本中，用户提供了 checkRole :: Proxy required -> RoleType required -> Bool，然后库函数 checkAuth 充当智能构造函数来构建适当的证明：

checkAuth :: CheckRole required => Proxy required -> RoleType required -> Maybe (Proof required)
checkAuth p role
  | checkRole p role = Just Proof
  | otherwise = Nothing

这使我们能够将证明数据构造函数保留在用户空间之外，以消除伪造。

在新世界里我们不需要这个。

让我们退一步思考一下我们需要什么证据。

  1. 路线所需的角色。
  2. 用户具有的角色。

1 是我们如何向处理程序发送证明，2 是授权用户的实际角色，它将证明与其检查的主题联系起来。

data Proof (required :: kr) (actual :: ka) where
  Proof :: Proof required actual

required 是路由所需的角色，actual 是经过身份验证的用户的角色。

现在我们可以完成 checkRole 签名，删除代理。

checkRole :: Sing (actual :: RoleType required) -> Maybe (Proof required actual)

我们可以写一个这样的实例：

instance CheckRole 'Editor where
  checkRole SViewer = Nothing
  checkRole SEditor = Just Proof
  checkRole SAdmin  = Just Proof

所需的角色是“Editor”，因此 Sing（需要 RoleType）评估为 SUserRole _ 表示经过身份验证的用户的实际角色。 如果是 SViewer，那么我们会拒绝，否则我们会生成一个专门用于证明“编辑器实际”的 Just Proof。

在SEditor分支中，实际专门用于“Editor Give us Proof”Editor“Editor”。

现在，如果我们在 SViewer 分支中构建一个证明，它将是 Proof 'Editor 'Viewer。 这在道德上是错误的，因为证明的重点是实际满足角色要求。 我们需要消除产生这种证明的能力。

我们可以用另一种类型的家庭来做到这一点。

type family Satisfied (required :: kr) (actual :: ka) :: Constraint

满足是当实际满足要求的满足条件时成立的约束。 该条件是用户定义的（相等、设置成员资格、顺序等），因此我将暂时推迟示例。

现在我们将这个约束附加到证明上，我们可以保证在构造证明时满足条件成立。


data Proof (required :: kr) (actual :: ka) where
  Proof :: (Satisfied required actual) => Proof required actual

现在 checkRole SViewer = Just Proof 将要求满意的“编辑器”查看器，而不会进行类型检查。

这也意味着我们不再需要担心隐藏 Proof 数据构造函数。 用户无法构造出其实际角色不满足要求的证明。 这样我们也可以去掉checkAuth库函数。

建设中

现在我们需要使用新的 CheckRole 类更新 HasServer 实例。 为此，我们需要根据请求值具体化角色类型。

我们使用另一个 GADT SomeRole 来完成此操作，它的工作方式与 SomeSBool 类似。

data SomeRole (authF :: k -> Type) where
  SomeRole :: Sing (r :: k) -> authF r -> SomeRole authF

唯一真正的区别是我们还将 SomeRole 与 auth 值以及单例一起打包。 我们在 AuthHandler 中构造 SomeRole 值，然后在 HasServer 实例中释放它。

介绍：

parseRole :: Request -> Maybe (SomeRole Authz)
parseRole req = case lookup "X-Role" (requestHeaders req) of
  Just "viewer" -> pure $ SomeRole SViewer (Authz "Reed")
  Just "editor" -> pure $ SomeRole SEditor (Authz "Lyxia")
  Just "admin" -> pure $ SomeRole SAdmin (Authz "Sandy")
  _ -> Nothing

-- | Reads the "X-Role" header to determine the user's role.
-- Missing header -> 401. Values: "viewer", "editor", "admin".
roleAuthHandler :: AuthHandler Request (SomeRole Authz)
roleAuthHandler = mkAuthHandler $ \req ->
  maybe (throwError err401) pure (parseRole req)

消除：

authCheck :: Request -> DelayedIO (Satisfies required authF)
authCheck request = do
  eResult <- liftIO $ runHandler (authHandler' request)
  case eResult of
    Left err -> delayedFailFatal err
    Right (SomeRole sActual auth) ->
      case checkRole sActual of
        Just proof -> pure $ Satisfies proof auth
        Nothing ->
          delayedFail
            err403
              { errBody = "Forbidden: insufficient permissions",
                errHeaders = [("Content-Type", "text/plain; charset=utf-8")]
              }

满足成为最终的 GADT，它携带关联的 Proof auth 对象以传播到我们的处理程序。

data Satisfies (required :: kr) (authF :: ka -> Type) where
  Satisfies :: Proof required actual -> authF actual -> Satisfies required authF

放电

我们仍然需要为 UserRole 定义 Satisfied 实例。

type family (a :: UserRole) <=? (b :: UserRole) :: Bool where
  'Viewer <=? _       = 'True
  'Editor <=? 'Viewer = 'False
  'Editor <=? _       = 'True
  'Admin  <=? 'Admin  = 'True
  'Admin  <=? _       = 'False

type instance Satisfied (required :: UserRole) (actual :: UserRole) =
  (required <=? actual) ~ 'True

<=? 是专门针对 UserRole 的类型级别 Ord。 Viewer 匹配任何内容，Editor 匹配自身，或者 Admin、Admin 匹配自身。 然后在满意中，如果需要<=？ 实际计算结果为 'True，则整个约束计算结果为 'True ~ 'True 并成立。

最后我们回到了我们的仆人管理员那里。 他们收到一个 Satisfies 'Admin Authz，其中包含同一 r 的 Proof 和 A​​uthz，因此该证明只能用在它所检查的 Authz 上。

这允许我们编写 banUser ，使其只能由授权用户在授权路径中使用。

banUser :: Proof 'Admin r -> Authz r -> String
banUser _prf auth = "banned by " <> userName auth

结论

哇，太残酷了。 编写依赖 Haskell 是痛苦的。 幸运的是，大量的代码都是样板文件，可以通过模板 haskell 自动化，我已经在我的 libaryservant-auth-roles 中完成了这一点。

完整的 UserRole 示例变为：

data UserRole = Viewer | Editor | Admin

$(deriveOrdRole ''UserRole)

newtype Authz (r :: UserRole) = Authz {userName :: String}

type instance AuthServerData (AuthProtect "role-auth") = SomeRole Authz

parseRole :: Request -> Maybe (SomeRole Authz)
parseRole req = case lookup "X-Role" (requestHeaders req) of
  Just "viewer" -> Just (someUserRole Viewer (Authz "Reed"))
  Just "editor" -> Just (someUserRole Editor (Authz "Lyxia"))
  Just "admin" -> Just (someUserRole Admin (Authz "Sandy"))
  _ -> Nothing

roleAuthHandler :: AuthHandler Request (SomeRole Authz)
roleAuthHandler = mkAuthHandler $ maybe (throwError err401) pure . parseRole

banUser :: (IsAtleastAdmin userAuth) => Authz userAuth -> String
banUser auth = "banned by " <> userName auth

type PanelAdminAPI = RequireRole "role-auth" 'Admin :> "panel" :> Get '[JSON] String

type PanelEditorAPI = RequireRole "role-auth" 'Editor :> "panel" :> Get '[JSON] String

type PanelViewerAPI = RequireRole "role-auth" 'Viewer :> "panel" :> Get '[JSON] String

type FallthroughAPI = PanelAdminAPI :<|> PanelEditorAPI :<|> PanelViewerAPI

server :: Server FallthroughAPI
server = pAdmin :<|> pEditor :<|> pViewer
  where
    pAdmin :: Satisfies 'Admin Authz -> Handler String
    pAdmin (Satisfies UserRoleProof auth) = pure (banUser auth)

    pEditor :: Satisfies 'Editor Authz -> Handler String
    pEditor _ = pure "editor panel"

    pViewer :: Satisfies 'Viewer Authz -> Handler String
    pViewer _ = pure "viewer panel"

app :: IO Application
app = pure $ serveWithContext (Proxy @FallthroughAPI) (roleAuthHandler :. EmptyContext) server

看不到单例或奇怪的样板！

层次结构是通过 Ord 派生的。 可以使用deriveEqRole 和deriveMemberRole 派生基于平等的角色和基于设置成员身份的角色。
